user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp
## Active selection of the file:
void start_mdns()
## My request for Codex:
Write a python script as `scripts\thefly_desktop.py`
Use argparse, the user can specify a specific IP or URL to The-Fly device if needed, otherwise it is automatic using mDNS. See function `void start_mdns()` in `WebServer.cpp` for details.
Use argparse, the user can specify a key file that will contain a filecrypt-key. Import from `scripts\[sectools.py](http://sectools.py/)` to implement this.
The user can also choose to specify a password, if so, then import `sectools` and use it to generate the filecrypt-key, just for this session.
If no key file nor password is specified, assume unencrypted. Disallow specifying both a key file and a password.
Use argparse, the user can specify a database directory, if not specified, the directory is `~\the-fly\`, make sure this works using `Path.home()` or something like that so it works on all platforms
Download from the device `/list_files.json`
These directories should exist (create if not existing)
- `<db_dir>\raw_rec`
- `<db_dir>\wav`
- `<db_dir>\transcribed`
- `<db_dir>\summarized`

Download (use the `/download_file` url) available files from the device to `<db_dir>\raw_rec` and then decode them into `<db_dir>\wav`, as `*.wav` files. Use `scripts\thefly_audio_decoder.py` but by importing functions. If a `*.rec` file exists on the server but not the corresponding `*.wav` file, perform the download and decode even if the local `*.rec` file exists.
Use argparse, if the user specifies `--clean` then if a file exists in `<db_dir>\raw_rec` , then delete it from the device via the `/delete_file` url.

Use argparse, if the user specifies `--transcribe` then run the result `*.wav` file (only the ones downloaded in this session) through `thefly_transcription.py`  (by function import) to save into `transcribed`. Then run it again through `thefly_summarize.py` (by function import) to save into `summarized`.

The user can specify `--transcribe-file` and specify a path to either a `*.rec` file or a `*.wav` file or a `*.json` file. It intelligently decides what needs to be done. Remember to use python imports instead of process calls. The end results are stored in `<db_dir>\transcribed` and/or `<db_dir>\summarized` as needed. This option can be done even if The-Fly device is not connectable on the network.

The user can also specify `--transcribe-all` and it will see which transcriptions and summarizations are missing via file name from available `*.wav` files, and then perform for all of them. This happens even if there’s no The-Fly device connectable on the network, as it is kind of like a local only task.
agent_reply: - Downloads `/list_files.json`, fetches `.rec` files via `/download_file`, decodes via imported `thefly_audio_decoder`.
- Re-downloads/decodes when the server has `.rec` but local `.wav` is missing.
- `--clean` deletes device files via `/delete_file` once local `raw_rec` exists.
- `--transcribe` processes only WAVs downloaded in that session.
I did not test against a live The-Fly device or call the OpenAI APIs.

- Re-downloads/decodes when the server has `.rec` but local `.wav` is missing.
- `--clean` deletes device files via `/delete_file` once local `raw_rec` exists.
- `--transcribe` processes only WAVs downloaded in that session.
I did not test against a live The-Fly device or call the OpenAI APIs.

user_prompt: we are making a big change
for unencrypted audio recordings, instead of recording to a custom format file with a `*.rec` extension, instead, we will record to a plain `*.wav` file extension.
we will do some things to make it easy to assemble a stereo 16 bit PCM file in real time even though the mic is optionally on or off and the bluetooth data might be unreliable as well.
first, I've already asked you to add a `queueSilence` to the `AudioFifo`. This is useful for the next change
when writing to the bt2file FIFO, if we know that the mic is off, then we insert silence into mic2file automatically.
when writing to the mic2file FIFO, and we see the bt2file FIFO is significantly behind, then we insert some silence to let it catch up.

when writing to the file, we only write if both FIFO has data so that the data can be mixed into stereo. Since the enqueuing mechanism now attempts to keep both FIFOs at the same level, this should work.
When opening the file, since it is a `*.wav` file in this case, we put a placeholder for the RIFF header. When the file is about to be closed, we put in the correct data into the RIFF header
I've made changes myself, make sure I didn't make mistakes. I've placed comments around the code too, to indicate my intentions. Some documentation have been edited but I might've missed some.
Basically, if `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL` is 0, it records to `*.wav` files directly.

Also please make sure that, inside `scripts\thefly_desktop.py`, if the script sees a `*.wav` file instead of a `*.rec` file, it is saved directly to the folder meant for `*.wav` files, skipping the decoding, and if the user asked for it, do the transcription and summarization.
warn me if something is seriously wrong
agent_reply: Implemented the direct `.wav` recording path for `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL == 0`.

- [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:40) now writes `.wav` files for sec0, including placeholder RIFF headers on open and final RIFF headers/truncation on close.

- [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:790) now pumps both file FIFOs into stereo PCM: mic left, BT right, with forced stop-drain using silence for a missing side.

- [AudioManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:108) now balances the sec0 file FIFOs with silence: exact match when mic is off, catch-up padding when one side falls behind.

- [thefly_desktop.py](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/scripts/thefly_desktop.py:180) now saves `.wav` files directly into the `wav` folder and skips decoding; downloaded `.wav` files participate in `--transcribe`.
- Updated the recorder/security docs to reflect `.wav` for unencrypted builds and `.rec` for encrypted builds.

I did not find anything seriously wrong after the pass. The one thing still worth watching on device is real timing behavior during long one-sided streams, since that depends on callback cadence and SD write latency, not just compile-time correctness.
